skill.name: axe-ios-simulator
skill.description: Guides iOS Simulator automation with AXe CLI for taps, gestures, text input, hardware buttons, screenshots, video capture, and accessibility inspection.
skill.summary: Automate iOS Simulator interactions and accessibility checks with AXe CLI commands.
skill.author: 0xBigBoss

security_audit.summary: The static external-command findings are true positives because the skill instructs users to install and run AXe, ffmpeg, grep, and shell loops. These commands are consistent with the stated iOS Simulator automation purpose, and no malicious network, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection evidence was found. Weak cryptography alerts appear to be false positives from terms such as keycodes and command names, while reconnaissance alerts are limited to simulator UI inspection.

medium_findings.title: External Command Guidance Controls Local Simulator
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill provides shell commands that install AXe and run AXe, ffmpeg, grep, and shell control flow. This is expected for simulator automation, but users should review commands before execution because they can interact with local simulator state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain direct command examples, so the external-command pattern is real. The same context shows intended simulator testing behavior rather than hidden execution or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Simulator Screen and Accessibility Data Capture
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill documents screenshots, video recording, video streaming, and accessibility tree inspection. These features can expose test data, typed values, or private simulator content if used on sensitive applications.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly capture images, video, streams, and UI descriptions. The privacy risk follows directly from the documented behavior, although no exfiltration path is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The referenced lines contain metadata, HID keycode examples, screenshot paths, accessibility inspection, and grep usage. No weak cryptographic algorithm, hashing operation, or encryption behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each cited line was reviewed and none references cryptographic primitives. The scanner likely matched generic terms such as key, output, or describe commands.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Simulator Scoped
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for host reconnaissance. The commands inspect or manipulate the iOS Simulator UI and accessibility tree. No evidence found of host inventory collection, network probing, persistence checks, or system credential discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed commands are consistent with UI automation and accessibility testing. The file contains no host-level reconnaissance commands beyond simulator-focused inspection.

low_findings.title: Example Credential Entry May Encourage Sensitive Test Data Use
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE as a minor content risk. The form-filling example types an email address and sample password into the simulator. Users should avoid real credentials in automated simulator sessions and recordings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example clearly includes a password-like value, but it appears to be placeholder test content. The risk is user behavior and data exposure, not confirmed secret theft.

dangerous_patterns.title: Homebrew Installation of External Binary
dangerous_patterns.description: The installation step asks users to install AXe through Homebrew. This is a normal setup step, but it introduces supply-chain trust in an external package.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and external package installation has inherent supply-chain risk. No malicious install flags or obfuscation were found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Video Streaming and Pipeline Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The video commands can stream simulator output and pipe frames to ffmpeg. Users should avoid capturing sensitive application states.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands directly record, stream, and pipe simulator video. The impact depends on what data is visible in the simulator.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Loop with UI Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: The automation pattern loops over UI inspection output and performs scrolling. This is useful, but automated loops can cause unintended simulator actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell loop is explicit and simulator-scoped. The risk is moderate because it automates repeated actions, but it does not target host systems.

content.user_title: Automate iOS Simulator Testing with AXe
content.value_statement: Manual Simulator checks slow mobile QA and accessibility review. This skill provides AXe commands for taps, text, screenshots, video, and UI inspection.
actual_capabilities: Lists simulators and uses a selected UDID for AXe commands.
Runs tap, swipe, gesture, touch, key, and hardware button actions.
Types text directly, from stdin, or from a local file.
Captures screenshots, records video, and streams simulator frames.
Inspects the accessibility tree for the full screen or a point.
Shows simple shell patterns for wait, scroll, form fill, and capture flows.
limitations: Requires AXe CLI, Homebrew setup, and a working iOS Simulator.
Does not provide an app testing framework or assertions by itself.
Coordinates and labels may need updates when the app UI changes.
Screenshots, video, and UI trees can expose sensitive simulator data.

use_cases.title: Mobile QA Smoke Checks
use_cases.description: Automate common taps, gestures, and screenshots during manual iOS Simulator verification.

use_cases.title: Accessibility Element Review
use_cases.description: Inspect labels, identifiers, and UI tree output while testing accessibility behavior.

use_cases.title: Developer Demo Capture
use_cases.description: Record short simulator videos or screenshots after scripted app interactions.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Tap
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one simulator action.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use axe-ios-simulator to show how I can list simulators, set the UDID, and tap a button by accessibility label.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a Test Form
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow with text input and navigation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use axe-ios-simulator to create a simulator form-filling flow using placeholder test data only.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Evidence After Interaction
prompt_templates.scenario: QA workflow with screenshot or video output.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use axe-ios-simulator to automate a tap, wait for the result, then capture a screenshot and short video.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Accessibility Search Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced loop that combines UI inspection and gestures.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use axe-ios-simulator to design a scroll-and-find workflow that inspects the UI tree before tapping a target element.

output_examples.input: Ask for a tap by accessibility label in the active simulator.
output_examples.output: A short command sequence that lists simulators, stores the UDID, and taps the requested labeled element.

output_examples.input: Ask for evidence capture after submitting a screen.
output_examples.output: A workflow that performs the submit action, waits for the UI to settle, and saves a screenshot or recording.

output_examples.input: Ask for accessibility inspection around a problem button.
output_examples.output: Guidance for reading the UI tree or a point-specific accessibility description before choosing a reliable selector.

best_practices: Use placeholder data when typing into forms or recording simulator sessions.
Prefer accessibility labels or identifiers over fixed coordinates when possible.
Review generated shell commands before running them on a local machine.
anti_patterns: Do not record screens that contain real credentials or customer data.
Do not rely on fixed coordinates for frequently changing app layouts.
Do not run installation or pipeline commands without checking the tools involved.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates iOS Simulator actions through AXe CLI examples for touch, text, buttons, media capture, and UI inspection.

faq.question: Does it install AXe automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents the Homebrew installation command, but the user chooses whether to run it.

faq.question: Can it test accessibility labels?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows commands for tapping by label or identifier and inspecting the accessibility tree.

faq.question: Can it replace a full test suite?
faq.answer: No. It provides automation commands, but it does not include assertions, reporting, or test runner integration.

faq.question: What data should I avoid using?
faq.answer: Avoid real passwords, customer records, tokens, private messages, and other sensitive information in captured simulator sessions.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
